requirements.english: TOEFL, IELTS, PTE, or Duolingo accepted with no minimum score; exempt if you studied 2+ years in the US or hold a degree taught in English.
requirements.admission_model_note: Students are admitted to the aerospace option by committee, not to a specific lab; they must select and be accepted by a research advisor by the end of the first year.

funding.note: All PhD students receive full tuition plus a living stipend (about $50,000/yr from Sept 2026) via fellowships, research, or teaching assistantships, guaranteed while making satisfactory progress.

requirements.contact_note: No official guidance; because admission is by committee with advisors chosen after enrollment, pre-application contact is not part of the stated process.

programs.data_currency: Researched 2026-07-05 from official pages — verify before applying.

faculty.name: H. Jane Bae
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Aerospace; Susan Wu Scholar
faculty.summary: Bae develops physics-based and data-driven models of near-wall turbulence to cut the cost of simulating high-Reynolds-number flows relevant to aircraft, wind farms, and atmospheric prediction. Recent work applies machine learning, reinforcement learning, and information theory to build wall models for large-eddy simulation, including a multi-agent reinforcement-learning wall model published in Nature Communications, and probes the self-sustaining process and streak dynamics of the buffer and logarithmic layers with minimal empirical input.

faculty.name: Adrián Lozano-Durán
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Aerospace
faculty.summary: Lozano-Durán studies turbulence through theory, high-fidelity simulation, and experiment. A signature theme applies causal inference and information theory to identify the dynamical mechanisms and energy transfers that sustain wall turbulence, then uses those insights with machine learning to build closure and reduced-order models for CFD. His target applications span low-speed aerodynamics through supersonic and hypersonic flows. He joined Caltech aerospace from MIT following a visiting-associate appointment, and continues to bridge fundamental turbulence physics with data-driven modeling.

faculty.name: Joanna Austin
faculty.title: Professor of Aerospace
faculty.summary: Austin leads an experimental group studying compressible high-speed fluid mechanics: hypervelocity flight and planetary entry, supersonic combustion and detonation, shock-bubble and shock-shock interactions, and hypersonic boundary-layer instabilities. The group operates major GALCIT facilities including the T5 free-piston reflected-shock tunnel, a hypervelocity expansion tube, and a Ludwieg tube, characterizing real-gas and thermochemical nonequilibrium effects relevant to atmospheric-entry vehicles and high-speed air-breathing propulsion.

faculty.name: Ares J. Rosakis
faculty.title: Theodore von Karman Professor of Aeronautics and Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Rosakis designs experiments elucidating the physics of dynamic fracture and catastrophic failure in metals, polymers, metallic glasses, composites, layered solids, and frictional interfaces. His laboratory-earthquake paradigm creates controlled, repeatable surrogate ruptures to study dynamic shear rupture and frictional sliding, demonstrating supershear rupture speeds and rupture propagating as cracks or pulses. Recent work in Nature and PNAS shows that slowly creeping faults are not immune to major ruptures and examines fluid-injection-induced rupture initiation and propagation.

faculty.name: Guruswami (Ravi) Ravichandran
faculty.title: John E. Goode, Jr., Professor of Aerospace and Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Ravichandran studies the deformation and failure of materials, emphasizing dynamic behavior, wave propagation, and micro/nano-mechanics. His group works on active materials and structures (ferroelectrics, shape-memory alloys, MEMS), composites, fracture and adhesion, biomaterials and cell mechanics, and experimental methods. Recent efforts include the dynamic strength of materials at high pressures and shock-driven material response. He is a National Academy of Engineering member and former Otis Booth Leadership Chair of Caltech's Division of Engineering and Applied Science.

faculty.name: Aaron D. Ames
faculty.title: Bren Professor of Mechanical and Civil Engineering, Control and Dynamical Systems, and Aerospace
faculty.summary: Ames develops formal, optimization-based control that provably guarantees safety via control barrier functions and stability via control Lyapunov functions, unified in real-time quadratic programs. His group demonstrates these methods on hardware: bipedal and humanoid robots achieving dynamic walking and running, quadrupeds, drones, and assistive exoskeletons that restore mobility. Recent work fuses the framework with machine learning (neural gaits, episodic learning) for safe locomotion in natural environments. He directs Caltech's Center for Autonomous Systems and Technologies (CAST).

faculty.name: Sergio Pellegrino
faculty.title: Joyce and Kent Kresa Professor of Aerospace and Civil Engineering; Director, Graduate Aerospace Laboratories
faculty.summary: Pellegrino works on the mechanics of lightweight structures: packaging, deployment, shape control, and stability for future space telescopes, spacecraft antennas, and space-based solar power. He co-leads the Caltech Space Solar Power Project, designing constellations of ultralight modular spacecraft that collect sunlight and wirelessly beam power to Earth; the core module is a giant coilable structure of paper-thin materials that elastically self-deploys in orbit. His group invents foldable ultrathin composites integrating photovoltaics and power-beaming electronics.

faculty.name: John O. Dabiri
faculty.title: Centennial Professor of Aeronautics and Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Dabiri studies fluid mechanics at the intersection of biology, renewable energy, and environmental flows. His work on jellyfish propulsion and vortex dynamics informs bio-inspired energy systems; drawing on fish-schooling hydrodynamics, he pioneered densely packed vertical-axis wind-turbine arrays and wake-steering for conventional wind farms. Related efforts span biohybrid robotic jellyfish for ocean sensing and cardiovascular flow diagnostics. He is a MacArthur Fellow and National Medal of Science laureate.

faculty.name: Michael H. Dickinson
faculty.title: Esther M. and Abe M. Zarem Professor of Bioengineering and Aeronautics
faculty.summary: Dickinson integrates aerodynamics, neuroscience, and biomechanics to explain how insects fly. Using high-speed imaging, custom flight rigs, genetically targeted muscle-activity microscopy, and machine learning, his lab recently mapped how the roughly twelve steering muscles of the fruit fly Drosophila actuate the wing hinge to produce agile maneuvers, analyzing tens of thousands of wingbeats. The group also builds state-space aerodynamic models of flapping flight and studies long-range navigation and sensory control, bridging model-organism neuroscience with powered flight.

faculty.name: Angkur J. D. Shaikeea
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Aerospace; Bren Scholar
faculty.summary: Shaikeea (PhD Cambridge 2022; joined Caltech 2025) studies the mechanics of 3D architected metamaterials and scalable additive manufacturing. He extended elastic fracture mechanics to truss-based metamaterials, showing their performance is limited by damage and defect tolerance, and derived design protocols for optimally robust discrete solids. His new lab builds advanced in-situ X-ray tools (tomography, hyperspectral imaging, ptychography, EDXRD, 3DXRD) to extract full 3D stress and strain fields under load, feeding data-driven models of material behavior.

faculty.name: Tim Colonius
faculty.title: Frank and Ora Lee Marble Professor of Mechanical Engineering and Medical Engineering
faculty.summary: Colonius leads the Computational and Data-Driven Fluid Dynamics group, using high-fidelity numerical simulation to study multiscale flow phenomena including jet aeroacoustics, flow instabilities, shock waves, and bubble dynamics. Recent work develops reduced-order models, data assimilation, and control theory to predict and manipulate flow structure, with applications spanning unsteady and bio-inspired aerodynamics, flow-energy harvesting, and jet-noise reduction. His group also applies ultrasound, shock waves, and cavitation to biomedical therapies such as lithotripsy, histotripsy, and targeted drug delivery.

faculty.name: Guillaume Blanquart
faculty.title: Professor of Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Blanquart models the coupling between combustion chemistry and turbulent flow using direct numerical simulation with detailed chemistry and transport. Recent research targets lean premixed hydrogen and hydrogen/air flames at high Karlovitz number, examining how intense turbulence reshapes flame structure, vorticity, and the two-way coupling between turbulence and chemical reactions. His group also studies pollutant and soot nano-particle formation, thermal diffusion effects, and hydrodynamic and flame instabilities, advancing predictive models relevant to cleaner combustion and next-generation propulsion.

faculty.name: Soon-Jo Chung
faculty.title: Bren Professor of Control and Dynamical Systems
faculty.summary: Chung directs the Autonomous Robotics and Control Lab, developing theory and flight systems for autonomous aerial, space, and underwater vehicles. His work spans learning-based control, estimation, planning, and navigation, with a signature focus on multi-agent swarm flight on Earth and in space, pursued jointly with JPL. Recent projects include neural-network control with stability guarantees and mission concepts such as inflatable regolith shielding for lunar landing. He received the 2024 IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters Best Paper Award.

faculty.name: Chiara Daraio
faculty.title: G. Bradford Jones Professor of Mechanical Engineering and Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Daraio designs materials whose micro- and meso-structure yields advanced mechanical and sensing functions, bridging mechanical engineering and applied physics. Her group engineers ordered and disordered mechanical metamaterials for soft robotics, wearable devices, and shock and vibration absorption, and develops acoustic imaging and thermal-sensing methods for health monitoring. Recent work applies machine learning to design disordered metamaterials and explores smart tunable fabrics and sustainable materials for packaging and construction, embedding computation and mechanical intelligence directly into matter.

faculty.name: Nadia Lapusta
faculty.title: Lawrence A. Hanson, Jr., Professor of Mechanical Engineering and Geophysics
faculty.summary: Lapusta studies the mechanics and physics of friction, fracture, and earthquake source processes, combining analytical theory with large-scale numerical modeling of seismic and aseismic fault slip. Recent work shows that slip velocity, more than total slip, governs how friction evolves during simulated earthquakes, and that interfaces which appear stationary continue to heal, raising subsequent sliding friction by roughly twenty percent after a year of contact. Her group links laboratory friction experiments to fault-scale models of geomaterial deformation.

faculty.name: Kaushik Bhattacharya
faculty.title: Howell N. Tyson, Sr., Professor of Mechanics and Materials Science
faculty.summary: Bhattacharya studies the mechanical behavior of solids at the intersection of mechanics, materials science, and applied mathematics, using theory to guide the design and directed discovery of new materials. His group works on active materials including shape-memory alloys, ferroelectrics, and liquid-crystal elastomers, and on heterogeneous materials and structures. Recent 2025 work includes light-induced deformation of photoactive shells, an artificial sunflower, and a phase-field framework for systematically designing compliant morphing structures. He also serves as Caltech's Vice Provost.

faculty.name: Morteza Gharib
faculty.title: Hans W. Liepmann Professor of Aeronautics and Medical Engineering
faculty.summary: Gharib, a longtime GALCIT director, conducts experimental fluid dynamics spanning vortex dynamics, active and passive flow control, and nano and micro fluid mechanics. His group develops advanced flow-imaging diagnostics and studies autonomous flight and underwater systems, alongside bio-inspired devices drawing on cardiovascular and animal-locomotion flows. Recent efforts include Mars aerial-vehicle concepts and vortex-based approaches to propulsion and energy. Elected to the National Academy of Engineering and awarded the APS Fluid Dynamics Prize, he bridges fundamental fluid physics with engineered and medical applications.

faculty.name: José E. Andrade
faculty.title: George W. Housner Professor of Civil and Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Andrade works in computational mechanics at the interface of physics and mechanics, building predictive multiscale models of granular and porous materials such as sand, snow, rock, concrete, and bone. His group connects micro-scale grain-to-grain force transmission with macro-scale continuum behavior, with applications in energy exploration and space missions, including regolith mechanics and planetary sampling. Recent work addresses the seismic performance of multiblock tower structures for gravity energy storage. He leads Caltech's computational geomechanics effort and received the Zienkiewicz Medal.

faculty.name: Paul E. Dimotakis
faculty.title: John K. Northrop Professor of Aeronautics and Professor of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Dimotakis conducts experimental and computational research on turbulent mixing and chemical reactions in subsonic and supersonic free-shear flows, including the mixing transition, scalar dispersion, and the geometry of interfaces in turbulence. His interests extend to hypersonic propulsion and high-speed endoatmospheric flight. Recent collaborations with JPL address remote sensing of the atmosphere from space and parachute dynamics for planetary entry, descent, and landing. A Fellow of APS, AAAS, and AIAA, he received the AIAA Fluid Dynamics Award for his mixing and combustion research.

faculty.name: Dan Meiron
faculty.title: Fletcher Jones Professor of Aeronautics and Applied and Computational Mathematics
faculty.summary: Meiron develops computational methods and models for fundamental fluid-mechanical phenomena, with particular emphasis on shock-driven flow instabilities, turbulence, and simulation of solid mechanics at high strain rates. Bridging aeronautics with applied and computational mathematics, his work supports large-scale, high-performance simulation of coupled multiphysics problems. He has held leadership roles in Caltech's predictive-science and computational research efforts, guiding uncertainty quantification and verification and validation for complex engineering systems. He serves as Caltech's graduate option representative for aeronautics and space engineering.

faculty.name: Michael Watkins
faculty.title: Professor of Aerospace and Geophysics; Director, Keck Institute for Space Studies
faculty.summary: Watkins works in aerospace and geophysics and directs the Keck Institute for Space Studies, which incubates new space missions and technologies with campus and JPL researchers. Over a 27-year JPL career, culminating as JPL director, he co-originated the GRACE satellite-gravimetry concept and served as project scientist for GRACE, GRACE Follow-On, and GRAIL, and as mission manager for the Curiosity Mars rover. His expertise spans satellite geodesy, precise orbit determination, and space-mission systems engineering. He is a member of the National Academy of Engineering.

faculty.name: Sandra M. Troian
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Physics, Aeronautics, and Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Troian heads the Laboratory of Interfacial and Small Scale Transport, studying micro- and nanoscale fluidic systems dominated by capillary, van der Waals, Maxwell, thermocapillary, and Marangoni surface forces. Her group combines fundamental analysis with engineering design of thin films, droplet manipulation, and spacecraft micropropulsion, controlling fluid transport through chemical patterning and temperature gradients rather than mechanical pumps. She is known for the Thompson-Troian slip condition for liquid flow at solid interfaces and for thermocapillary methods of directing microscopic droplets.

requirements.english: TOEFL iBT 94+ (19+ per section) or IELTS 7.0+ overall; exemptions for degrees from the US or qualifying English-speaking countries. MyBest scores not accepted.
requirements.admission_model_note: Students are admitted by the graduate committee, then confirm a thesis advisor during the first month; those seeking GRA funding typically arrange support with a specific faculty member.

funding.note: About 90% of PhD students are funded via GRA or GTA with out-of-state tuition waivers; support is typically renewed yearly with satisfactory progress.

requirements.contact_note: Contacting faculty directly is encouraged to secure GRA/research-assistant support.

programs.data_currency: Researched 2026-07-05 from official pages — verify before applying.

faculty.name: Cyrus K. Aidun
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Aidun develops and applies computational and experimental methods for multiphase and particulate suspension flows. Recent work couples high-fidelity numerical models, including lattice-Boltzmann approaches, with high-performance computing to resolve particle-fluid interactions, interfacial transport, and dense suspension dynamics, with applications spanning biological cellular and blood-flow transport and industrial process flows. He integrates modeling, theory, and experiment to link microscale particle dynamics to macroscopic rheology, targeting predictive, physics-based simulation of complex suspensions across scales.

faculty.name: Tim Lieuwen
faculty.title: Regents' Professor; David S. Lewis, Jr. Chair
faculty.summary: Lieuwen studies clean combustion and propulsion, combustion dynamics and instabilities, acoustics, and the utilization of alternative and low-carbon fuels such as hydrogen in gas-turbine and energy systems. Recent activity emphasizes resilient clean-energy systems and the fundamental physics of unsteady, fuel-flexible combustion. He directs Georgia Tech's Strategic Energy Institute, has authored or edited multiple combustion books including Unsteady Combustor Physics and over 400 papers, and received the 2025 ASME Medal.

faculty.name: Ye Zhao
faculty.title: Associate Professor; Woodruff Faculty Fellow
faculty.summary: Zhao directs the Laboratory for Intelligent Decision and Autonomous Robots, developing planning, control, decision-making, and learning for highly dynamic, underactuated, and human-cooperative robots in unstructured environments. Recent projects include perturbation-resilient bipedal locomotion via model-predictive control, humanoid loco-manipulation through imitation learning, traversability estimation for rough terrain, and socially and emotion-aware navigation via deep reinforcement learning. His work bridges optimization-based control and learning to enable agile legged robots. He holds an NSF CAREER award.

faculty.name: Satish Kumar
faculty.title: Rae S. and Frank H. Neely Professor
faculty.summary: Kumar works on micro- and nanoscale electrical and thermal transport in microelectronics and nano-engineered systems, thermal management, and high-precision thermal metrology. Recent work applies physics-informed neural networks to phase-change problems, machine-learning-enabled thermal-property inversion, microfluidic cooling for computing processors, and electric-motor thermal management, plus DARPA-funded high-temperature materials for hypersonics. His group targets the thermal bottlenecks limiting next-generation electronics and propulsion, blending multi-physics transport modeling with machine learning and metrology.

faculty.name: Shreyes N. Melkote
faculty.title: Morris M. Bryan, Jr. Professor; Interim Chair, Woodruff School
faculty.summary: Melkote studies the mechanics and dynamics of manufacturing processes to improve accuracy and efficiency through tool-material interactions. Recent projects include deep-learning approaches to supplier selection and machining-sequence planning, bimetallic interface engineering via directed-energy deposition, and deflection-limited trajectory planning for robotic milling. His group spans precision machining, industrial robotics, and data-driven manufacturing, with over 300 publications. He serves as Interim Chair of the Woodruff School and Executive Director of the Novelis Innovation Hub.

faculty.name: Antonia Antoniou
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Antoniou investigates structure-property-performance relationships using experimental mechanics, materials characterization, and data-driven analysis to understand material reliability under mechanical and environmental loading. Recent work covers digital image correlation methods, nanocomposite and conductive inks, and characterization of flexible and functional material systems, bridging theoretical analysis with experimental validation to improve system reliability. Her research connects micro-scale deformation mechanisms to macroscopic durability in emerging material systems.

faculty.name: Ting Zhu
faculty.title: Professor; Woodruff Faculty Fellow
faculty.summary: Zhu models the mechanical behavior of advanced structural and energy materials from nano- to macro-scale using atomistic, continuum, and multiscale simulation to uncover mechanisms of deformation, damage, and failure. Current topics include ultra-strength and 3D-printed metal alloys, strengthening in high-entropy alloys, electrode degradation in lithium-ion batteries, fracture in 2D materials, irradiation damage, hydrogen embrittlement, and nanostructured shape-memory alloys. He received the 2023 ASME Centennial Mid-Career Award.

faculty.name: Costas D. Arvanitis
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Arvanitis uses acoustic cavitation and wave physics to build precise, feedback-controlled focused-ultrasound systems that modulate biological function noninvasively. Recent work focuses on brain applications: transient blood-brain-barrier opening for targeted cell, gene, and drug delivery, cancer immunotherapy, and augmenting immune-cell trafficking into tumors. He combines microbubble dynamics, thermal and mechanical bioeffects, and closed-loop control, developing integrated imaging and therapy platforms that translate ultrasound-based treatments for neurological disease toward the clinic.

faculty.name: Yuhang Hu
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Hu studies soft active materials that combine solid and liquid components, such as gels, integrating mechanics with materials chemistry. Her work spans fundamental chemomechanical modeling, multi-scale experimental characterization, and design of dynamic, adaptive, multifunctional responsive materials. She extends these principles to soft living systems including cells, tissues, and organs, developing methods to measure and predict the coupled mechanical and transport behavior of hydrogels.

programs.university: ETH Zürich (Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Zurich)

requirements.deadline_display: Rolling — no fixed deadline (positions advertised year-round)
requirements.english: No formal TOEFL/IELTS required for the doctorate; strong written and spoken English proficiency is expected.
requirements.admission_model_note: You must first secure a professor with funding who agrees to supervise you, then register; the D-MAVT doctoral committee formally approves admission after consultation with the supervisor.

funding.note: Doctoral students are salaried scientific assistants (roughly CHF 61,000+ gross in year one, rising annually), not tuition-payers; SNSF-funded contracts cap at four years.

requirements.contact_note: Emailing prospective supervisors directly is the primary and expected application pathway.

programs.data_currency: Researched 2026-07-05 from official pages — verify before applying.

faculty.name: Filippo Coletti
faculty.title: Full Professor of Experimental Fluid Dynamics
faculty.summary: Coletti's group uses advanced imaging to study the fundamental dynamics of flowing matter across scales. Recent thrusts include particle-laden and multiphase turbulence, the subject of his 2022 Annual Review of Fluid Mechanics article, aerosol dispersal and transport, snow and precipitation physics, and microscale biomedical flows such as hepatic microcirculation, plus ocean-microplastics-relevant studies. He holds an ERC Consolidator Grant and was named an APS Fellow in 2025 for contributions to particle-laden turbulence.

faculty.name: Marco Hutter
faculty.title: Full Professor of Robotic Systems
faculty.summary: Hutter leads a leading legged-robotics group built around the ANYmal quadruped. Landmark recent work includes learning robust perceptive locomotion in the wild, training walking policies in minutes via massively parallel deep reinforcement learning, perceptive locomotion via nonlinear model-predictive control, and ANYmal Parkour agile navigation. The group builds simulation frameworks and deploys robots for industrial inspection, DARPA SubT search-and-rescue, and autonomous forest inventory. Its lab open-positions page states it continuously seeks excellent PhD students.

faculty.name: Melanie N. Zeilinger
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Intelligent Control Systems
faculty.summary: Zeilinger works at the intersection of control theory and machine learning, emphasizing safety and performance guarantees for learning-based systems. Recent contributions include predictive safety filters for constrained nonlinear systems, a unifying view of data-driven safety across Hamilton-Jacobi reachability, control barrier functions, and predictive control, robust moving-horizon estimation, and active-learning-based MPC. Work from 2024-2025 extends to MPC for mobile-robot navigation in cluttered environments and distributed coverage control, with autonomous miniature race cars as a testbed.

faculty.name: Laura De Lorenzis
faculty.title: Professor of Computational Mechanics
faculty.summary: De Lorenzis develops computational methods for solid mechanics. A central recent thrust is automated, data-driven discovery of material models via her EUCLID framework, performing simultaneous model selection and parameter identification. She continues advancing variational phase-field models for brittle, anisotropic, and cohesive fracture and fatigue, plus hybrid physics-based and physics-informed deep-learning approaches. She is an editor of Computer Methods in Applied Mechanics and Engineering and currently Deputy Head of D-MAVT, and a EUROMECH Solid Mechanics Fellow.

faculty.name: Dennis M. Kochmann
faculty.title: Professor of Mechanics and Materials; Head of D-MAVT
faculty.summary: Kochmann links material microstructure to macroscopic behavior through theory, computation, and experiment, exploiting instabilities across scales. Recent highlights center on inverse design of nonlinear mechanical metamaterials via generative machine learning, including a 2023 Nature Machine Intelligence paper using video-diffusion models and a Nature Communications paper optimizing truss metamaterials by generative modeling. Newer work couples diffusion transformers with an algebraic language for 3D geometries. He currently serves as Head of D-MAVT.

faculty.name: Edoardo Mazza
faculty.title: Professor of Mechanics
faculty.summary: Mazza experimentally characterizes soft biological tissues, which are inhomogeneous, viscoelastic, anisotropic, and undergo large deformation, and describes them with nonlinear viscoelastic constitutive models across scales. Recent work includes in-vivo skin biomechanics linked to microstructure and pathology, shear-wave elastography of the pregnant uterine cervix showing reduced wave speed in pre-term-labor patients, reactive viscoelastic cervical-remodeling models, and mechanobiology using 3D in-vitro bioreactors. He also studies the mechanical biocompatibility of mesh implants and stents.

faculty.name: Nicolas Noiray
faculty.title: Full Professor of Combustion, Acoustics and Flow Physics
faculty.summary: Noiray's CAPS laboratory studies combustion instabilities and their control in power and propulsion systems, with a strong recent pivot to hydrogen and low-carbon fuels. Work includes real-time digital twins of azimuthal thermoacoustics in hydrogen-fired annular combustors, intrinsic aeroacoustic instabilities in can-annular gas turbines, and stochastic, nonlinear-dynamics modeling of flame-acoustic coupling. He also develops acoustic metamaterials, flow sensors and actuators, and passive and active control for cleaner hydrogen burners, supported by ERC Consolidator and Synergy grants.

faculty.name: Kristina Shea
faculty.title: Professor of Engineering Design and Computing
faculty.summary: Shea's Engineering Design and Computing Laboratory develops computational models, methods, and tools to automate the design and fabrication of complex optimized engineered systems. Recent work centers on computational design synthesis for 3D and 4D printing, including multi-state 4D-printed architected active composites, redeployable 4D-printed wave-spring actuators, and design-for-additive-manufacturing of architected lattices and mechanical metamaterials. Themes include design heuristics, generative and data-driven design, and reconfigurable stimuli-responsive structures, extending to soft and aquatic robotics.

faculty.name: André Bardow
faculty.title: Full Professor of Energy and Process Systems Engineering
faculty.summary: Bardow's Energy and Process Systems Engineering group designs sustainable energy and process systems, integrating molecular-to-systems-scale modeling with environmental life-cycle assessment. Recent work evaluates carbon-capture, utilization, and storage supply chains for reaching net-zero greenhouse-gas emissions, including a 2024 Nature paper on a platform to accelerate sorbent-based carbon capture and density-functional-theory prediction of CO2-capture properties of metal-organic frameworks. Other themes include power-to-X, the circular carbon economy, and computer-aided optimization of low-carbon fuels and refrigerants.

faculty.name: Bradley J. Nelson
faculty.title: Professor of Robotics and Intelligent Systems
faculty.summary: Nelson's Multi-Scale Robotics Lab builds millimeter- to nanometer-scale intelligent machines for biomedical use, focusing on wirelessly powered magnetic microrobots. Recent advances include a clinically oriented magnetically guided microrobot platform integrating navigation, therapeutic delivery, and imaging, with multiple locomotion modes such as vessel-wall rolling, gradient pulling, and in-flow navigation to reach targets in endovascular and cerebrospinal spaces while carrying thrombolytic, antibiotic, or tumor drugs. Related work covers electromagnetic navigation systems for operating theatres and remote magnetic telesurgery.

faculty.name: Hamsa Balakrishnan
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (William E. Leonhard Professor)
faculty.summary: Works on the design and operation of large-scale cyber-physical infrastructures, especially air transportation systems and multi-agent autonomy.

faculty.name: Kerri Cahoy
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Sheila Evans Widnall Professor)
faculty.summary: Leads the STAR Lab developing small spacecraft, laser communications, and space-based sensing systems.

faculty.name: Luca Carlone
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics
faculty.summary: Leads the SPARK Lab on robot perception, simultaneous localization and mapping, and spatial understanding for autonomous systems.

faculty.name: Zachary Cordero
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Esther and Harold E. Edgerton Professor)
faculty.summary: Esther and Harold E. Edgerton Professor researching processing and mechanical behavior of aerospace materials and metal additive manufacturing.

faculty.name: Edward F. Crawley
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Ford Foundation Professor of Engineering)
faculty.summary: Ford Foundation Professor of Engineering researching the architecture and design of complex aerospace and engineering systems.

faculty.name: David L. Darmofal
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Jerome B. Wiesner Professor)
faculty.summary: Jerome B. Wiesner Professor working on computational fluid dynamics, mesh adaptation, and higher-order numerical methods for aerodynamics.

faculty.name: Olivier L. de Weck
faculty.title: Professor of Astronautics and Engineering Systems (Apollo Program Professor); Associate Department Head
faculty.summary: Apollo Program Professor and Associate Head of AeroAstro, researching systems engineering and complex engineered systems design.

faculty.name: Mark Drela
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Terry J. Kohler Professor)
faculty.summary: Terry J. Kohler Professor known for aerodynamic analysis and design methods and widely used tools such as XFOIL and AVL.

faculty.name: Edward M. Greitzer
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (H. N. Slater Professor)
faculty.summary: H. N. Slater Professor researching internal flows, propulsion systems, and turbomachinery fluid dynamics.

faculty.name: Carmen Guerra-Garcia
faculty.title: Esther and Harold E. Edgerton Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studying plasma-assisted combustion, atmospheric electricity and lightning physics, and plasma-based space propulsion systems.

faculty.name: Steven R. Hall
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Richard Cockburn Maclaurin Professor)
faculty.summary: Richard Cockburn Maclaurin Professor working on control systems, estimation, and their aerospace applications.

faculty.name: R. John Hansman
faculty.title: T. Wilson (1953) Professor
faculty.summary: Leading researcher in air transportation systems, studying human factors in aviation, flight deck automation, and air traffic management.

faculty.name: Wesley L. Harris
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Charles Stark Draper Professor)
faculty.summary: Charles Stark Draper Professor researching unsteady aerodynamics, aeroacoustics, and rarefied gas dynamics.

faculty.name: Daniel E. Hastings
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Cecil and Ida Green Professor in Education)
faculty.summary: Cecil and Ida Green Professor working on spacecraft-environment interactions, space systems engineering, and space policy.

faculty.name: Jonathan P. How
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Ford Professor of Engineering)
faculty.summary: Ford Professor of Engineering leading the Aerospace Controls Laboratory on decision-making and control for autonomous vehicles and multi-agent systems.

faculty.name: Nancy Leveson
faculty.title: Jerome C. Hunsaker Professor
faculty.summary: Creator of STAMP (System-Theoretic Accident Model and Processes), transforming system safety engineering for complex aerospace and software systems.

faculty.name: Paulo Lozano
faculty.title: Miguel Aleman Velasco Professor
faculty.summary: Director of the Space Propulsion Laboratory, developing microfabricated ion electrospray propulsion systems for small satellites and CubeSats.

faculty.name: Youssef M. Marzouk
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Breene M. Kerr Professor)
faculty.summary: Breene M. Kerr Professor working on uncertainty quantification, Bayesian inference, and inverse problems for computational science and engineering.

faculty.name: Dava J. Newman
faculty.title: Professor of Astronautics and Engineering Systems (Apollo Program Professor)
faculty.summary: Apollo Program Professor and director of the MIT Media Lab, researching human spaceflight, astronaut performance, and advanced spacesuit design.

faculty.name: Jaime Peraire
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (H. N. Slater Professor)
faculty.summary: H. N. Slater Professor developing computational methods and numerical schemes for fluid and solid mechanics.

faculty.name: Raul Radovitzky
faculty.title: Jerome C. Hunsaker Professor
faculty.summary: Developing computational multiscale methods for solid mechanics, studying impact dynamics, penetration, and failure of aerospace materials.

faculty.name: Nicholas Roy
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Jerome C. Hunsaker Professor)
faculty.summary: Jerome C. Hunsaker Professor working on robotics, machine learning, and autonomous decision-making under uncertainty.

faculty.name: Sara Seager
faculty.title: Class of 1941 Professor (Joint with EAPS)
faculty.summary: World-renowned exoplanet scientist characterizing exoplanet atmospheres and leading the search for biosignature gases on potentially habitable worlds.

faculty.name: Julie Shah
faculty.title: H.N. Slater Professor; Department Head
faculty.summary: Department head and pioneer in human-robot collaboration, developing AI systems that enable robots to work safely and efficiently alongside humans.

faculty.name: Zoltan Spakovszky
faculty.title: T. Wilson (1953) Professor
faculty.summary: Director of the Gas Turbine Laboratory, studying unsteady flows in turbomachinery, compressor stability, and advanced propulsion for sustainable aviation.

faculty.name: Julie A. Shah
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (H. N. Slater Professor); Department Head
faculty.summary: H. N. Slater Professor and Head of the Department of Aeronautics and Astronautics, working on human-robot collaboration and interactive AI systems.

faculty.name: R. John Hansman Jr.
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (T. A. Wilson Professor in Aeronautics)
faculty.summary: Directs the International Center for Air Transportation, studying aviation systems, flight operations, and human-automation interaction.

faculty.name: Sertac Karaman
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics
faculty.summary: Works on robotics and autonomy, including motion planning, perception, and high-speed autonomous navigation.

faculty.name: Nancy G. Leveson
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Jerome C. Hunsaker Professor)
faculty.summary: Jerome C. Hunsaker Professor and a founder of the field of system safety, developing the STAMP/STPA approach to safety of complex socio-technical systems.

faculty.name: Paulo C. Lozano
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (M. Alemán-Velasco Professor)
faculty.summary: Directs the Space Propulsion Laboratory developing electrospray and electric micropropulsion systems for small spacecraft.

faculty.name: David A. Mindell
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Frances and David Dibner Professor in the History of Engineering and Manufacturing)
faculty.summary: Holds appointments in AeroAstro and the history of engineering, studying human-machine relationships in autonomy, spaceflight, and exploration.

faculty.name: Eytan H. Modiano
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Richard Cockburn Maclaurin Professor)
faculty.summary: Richard Cockburn Maclaurin Professor researching communication and network theory, including wireless, optical, and satellite networks.

faculty.name: Raúl Radovitzky
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Jerome C. Hunsaker Professor)
faculty.summary: Jerome C. Hunsaker Professor researching computational modeling of solids and structures under extreme loading and impact.

faculty.name: Zoltán S. Spakovszky
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (T. A. Wilson Professor)
faculty.summary: T. A. Wilson Professor and director of the Gas Turbine Laboratory studying turbomachinery, propulsion, and aeroacoustics.

faculty.name: Russell L. Tedrake
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Toyota Professor; joint with EECS and Mechanical Engineering)
faculty.summary: Toyota Professor working on control, motion planning, and manipulation for robots, with appointments across AeroAstro, EECS, and Mechanical Engineering.

faculty.name: Ian A. Waitz
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Jerome C. Hunsaker Professor)
faculty.summary: Jerome C. Hunsaker Professor researching propulsion and the environmental impacts of aviation, including emissions and noise.

faculty.name: Brian L. Wardle
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Apollo Program Professor; joint with Mechanical Engineering)
faculty.summary: Apollo Program Professor leading research on advanced composites and nanoengineered aerospace materials and structures.

faculty.name: Brian C. Williams
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics
faculty.summary: Leads the Model-based Embedded and Robotic Systems group on AI for autonomous and self-diagnosing aerospace systems.

faculty.name: Moe Z. Win
faculty.title: Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Robert R. Taylor Professor)
faculty.summary: Robert R. Taylor Professor researching wireless communications, network localization, and stochastic network theory.

faculty.name: Chuchu Fan
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Leonardo Career Development Professor in Engineering)
faculty.summary: Leads the Reliable Autonomous Systems Lab on control, learning, and formal verification for safe autonomous systems.

faculty.name: Carmen Guerra-García
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Esther and Harold E. Edgerton Career Development Professor)
faculty.summary: Researches plasma physics and gas discharges with applications to aerospace systems and electrified aircraft.

faculty.name: Richard Linares
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics
faculty.summary: Works on astrodynamics, space situational awareness, and machine learning for satellite tracking and space traffic management.

faculty.name: Zachary Manchester
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Boeing Career Development Professor)
faculty.summary: Leads the Robotic Exploration Lab on control and optimization for agile robots and small spacecraft.

faculty.name: Lonnie Petersen
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Samuel A. Goldblith Professor of Applied Biology)
faculty.summary: Physician-scientist researching human physiology in spaceflight and aerospace medicine, with a joint appointment in the Institute for Medical Engineering and Science.

faculty.name: Qiqi Wang
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics
faculty.summary: Researches computational fluid dynamics, sensitivity analysis of chaotic and turbulent flows, and simulation-based optimization.

faculty.name: Andreea Bobu
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Boeing Career Development Professor)
faculty.summary: Assistant Professor researching robot learning and human-robot interaction, including learning aligned representations from human input.

faculty.name: Masha Folk
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Charles Stark Draper Professor)
faculty.summary: Assistant Professor researching propulsion systems, gas turbine engines, and combustion.

faculty.name: Jake Hecla
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Charles Stark Draper Career Development Professor)
faculty.summary: Assistant Professor researching radiation detection, gamma-ray imaging, and nuclear instrumentation for security and space applications.

faculty.name: Daniel Varon
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics (Boeing Career Development Professor)
faculty.summary: Assistant Professor researching satellite remote sensing of the atmosphere, including greenhouse-gas and methane monitoring.

faculty.name: Danielle Wood
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics and Media Arts and Sciences (Benesse Career Development Professor)
faculty.summary: Leads the Space Enabled research group applying space technology and systems to advance justice and sustainable development.
